channel.title: DJ's Website – Informatics
channel.description: Recent content in Informatics on DJ's Website

item.title: Docs: InChI Converter
item.description:
# import packages 
import os

# memory management 
import gc 
gc.collect()

2512

0. Start with InChI

InChI_Molecule = "InChI=1S/C13H20OS/c1-9(2)8-15-13-6-5-10(3)7-12(13)11(4)14/h5-7,9,11,14H,8H2,1-4H3"

1. Draw Molecule using InChI

from rdkit.Chem import Draw,MolFromInchi
import cv2
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

mol = MolFromInchi(InChI_Molecule)
display(Draw.MolToImage(mol))

2 From InChI to 3D Model

import pubchempy as pcp
from pubchempy import get_cids

cids = pcp.get_cids(InChI_Molecule, 'inchi')
print(cids)
### Format PubChem CID 
cid_for_query = f'cid:{cids[0]}'
print(cid_for_query)

cid:82265033

3D Model

import py3Dmol
view = py3Dmol.view(width=680, height=300, query=cid_for_query, linked=False)
view.setStyle({'stick': {}})
view.setBackgroundColor('#f9f4fb')
view.show()

You appear to be running in JupyterLab (or JavaScript failed to load for some other reason). You need to install the 3dmol extension:
jupyter labextension install jupyterlab_3dmol

3. InChI and InChIKey

InChI to InChI Key

HOST = "http://www.chemspider.com"
Operation = "/InChI.asmx/InChIToInChIKey?inchi="

import requests
request = requests.get('{}{}{}'.format(HOST, Operation, InChI_Molecule))
if request.ok:
    res = str(request.text.replace('<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>\r\n<string xmlns="http://www.chemspider.com/">', '').replace('</string>', '').strip())
else:
    print ("You need to provide a valid inchi!")
print(res)

MAPOVNJUSHTONP-UHFFFAOYSA-N

InChIKey to InChI

# go back from InChIKey to InChI
from chembl_webresource_client.unichem import unichem_client as unichem
ret = unichem.inchiFromKey(res)
print (ret[0]['standardinchi'])


InChI=1S/C13H20OS/c1-9(2)8-15-13-6-5-10(3)7-12(13)11(4)14/h5-7,9,11,14H,8H2,1-4H3

4. InChI Key to Smiles

# Now let us go to SMILES using another service for FUN 
import requests
def get_smiles_from_inchikey(inchikey):
    request = requests.get(f'https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/rest/pug/compound/inchikey/{inchikey}/property/CanonicalSMILES/JSON').json()
    return request['PropertyTable']['Properties'][0]['CanonicalSMILES']

smiles_pres=get_smiles_from_inchikey(res)
print(smiles_pres)

CC1=CC(=C(C=C1)SCC(C)C)C(C)O

Draw from Smiles

from rdkit import Chem
from rdkit.Chem import Draw
g = Chem.MolFromSmiles(smiles_pres)
Draw.MolToMPL(g, size=(200, 200))

Smiles to InChI

smiles_pres= "CC1=CC(=C(C=C1)SCC(C)C)C(C)O"
mol =Chem.MolFromSmiles(smiles_pres)

InChI_Molecule = Chem.inchi.MolToInchi(mol)
print(InChI_Molecule)

InChI=1S/C13H20OS/c1-9(2)8-15-13-6-5-10(3)7-12(13)11(4)14/h5-7,9,11,14H,8H2,1-4H3

5. InChI and Graph

InChI to Graph

import networkx as nx
from rdkit import Chem

def mol_to_nx(mol: Chem.Mol) -> nx.Graph:
    """
    Code from https://github.com/dakoner/keras-molecules
    """
    G = nx.Graph()

    for atom in mol.GetAtoms():
        G.add_node(atom.GetIdx(),
                   atomic_num=atom.GetAtomicNum(),
                   formal_charge=atom.GetFormalCharge(),
                   chiral_tag=atom.GetChiralTag(),
                   hybridization=atom.GetHybridization(),
                   num_explicit_hs=atom.GetNumExplicitHs(),
                   is_aromatic=atom.GetIsAromatic())
    for bond in mol.GetBonds():
        G.add_edge(bond.GetBeginAtomIdx(),
                   bond.GetEndAtomIdx(),
                   bond_type=bond.GetBondType())
    return G

def nx_to_mol(G: nx.Graph) -> Chem.Mol:
    mol = Chem.RWMol()
    atomic_nums = nx.get_node_attributes(G, 'atomic_num')
    chiral_tags = nx.get_node_attributes(G, 'chiral_tag')
    formal_charges = nx.get_node_attributes(G, 'formal_charge')
    node_is_aromatics = nx.get_node_attributes(G, 'is_aromatic')
    node_hybridizations = nx.get_node_attributes(G, 'hybridization')
    num_explicit_hss = nx.get_node_attributes(G, 'num_explicit_hs')
    node_to_idx = {}
    for node in G.nodes():
        a=Chem.Atom(atomic_nums[node])
        a.SetChiralTag(chiral_tags[node])
        a.SetFormalCharge(formal_charges[node])
        a.SetIsAromatic(node_is_aromatics[node])
        a.SetHybridization(node_hybridizations[node])
        a.SetNumExplicitHs(num_explicit_hss[node])
        idx = mol.AddAtom(a)
        node_to_idx[node] = idx

    bond_types = nx.get_edge_attributes(G, 'bond_type')
    for edge in G.edges():
        first, second = edge
        ifirst = node_to_idx[first]
        isecond = node_to_idx[second]
        bond_type = bond_types[first, second]
        mol.AddBond(ifirst, isecond, bond_type)

    Chem.SanitizeMol(mol)
    return mol

InChI_Molecule ="InChI=1S/C13H20OS/c1-9(2)8-15-13-6-5-10(3)7-12(13)11(4)14/h5-7,9,11,14H,8H2,1-4H3"
mol = Chem.MolFromInchi(InChI_Molecule)
graph = mol_to_nx(mol)

nx.draw(graph)

Graph to InChI

restored_inchi = Chem.MolToInchi(nx_to_mol(graph))
print(restored_inchi)

InChI=1S/C13H20OS/c1-9(2)8-15-13-6-5-10(3)7-12(13)11(4)14/h5-7,9,11,14H,8H2,1-4H3

6. Extracting Murcko Scaffold — The Molecule Backbone

from rdkit.Chem.Scaffolds import MurckoScaffold

Extracting Murcko Scaffold using InChI

def get_basic_marcko_scaffold(inchi):
    return MurckoScaffold.MurckoScaffoldSmiles(mol=Chem.MolFromInchi(inchi),includeChirality=False)

InChI_Molecule ="InChI=1S/C13H20OS/c1-9(2)8-15-13-6-5-10(3)7-12(13)11(4)14/h5-7,9,11,14H,8H2,1-4H3"
get_basic_marcko_scaffold(InChI_Molecule )

'c1ccccc1'

Extracting Murcko Scaffold using Smiles

def get_bm_scaffold(smiles):
    try:
        scaffold = Chem.MolToSmiles(MurckoScaffold.MakeScaffoldGeneric(mol=Chem.MolFromSmiles(smiles)))
    except Exception:
        #  When extraction of BM scaffold fails, return basic Murcko Scaffold in this notebook. 
        print("Raise AtomValenceException, return basic Murcko Scaffold")
        scaffold = smiles
    return scaffold


smiles_pres= "CC1=CC(=C(C=C1)SCC(C)C)C(C)O"
get_bm_scaffold(smiles_pres)

'CC(C)CCC1CCC(C)CC1C(C)C'

item.title: Docs: InChI
item.description:
What is InChI?

  • The IUPAC International Chemical Identifier is a textual identifier for chemical substances, designed to provide a standard way to encode molecular information and to facilitate the search for such information in databases and on the web.
  • Initially developed by IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) and NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) from 2000 to 2005, the format and algorithms are non-proprietary.
  • Example: InChI=1S/C13H20OS/c1-9(2)8-15-13-6-5-10(3)7-12(13)11(4)14/h5-7,9,11,14H,8H2,1-4H3

InChI Strucutre

1. InCHI starts with “InChI=”

2. Followed by Version

  • Version 1 for the datasets

3. “S” stands for “Standard InChI”

  • maintaining the same level of attention to structure details and the same conventions for drawing perception

4. Remainings are layers and sublayers

  • are seperated by “/”
  • start with a prefix character
  • there are six important layers

4.1 Main layer

4.1.1 Chemical Formula

  • Available in every InChI
  • No prefix character

4.1.2 Atom connections

  • Prefix: c
  • Describes which atoms are connected by bonds to which other ones
  • In this sublayer, atoms in the chemical formula are numbered in sequence
    • Except for hydrogens

4.1.3 Hydrogen atoms

  • Prefix: h
  • Describes how many hydrogen atoms are connected to each of the other atoms
  • May not always exist

4.2 Charge layer

4.2.1 Charge sublayer

  • Prefix: q

4.2.2 Proton sublayer

  • Prefix: p

4.3 Stereochemical layer

4.3.1 Double bonds and cumulenes

  • Prefix: b

4.3.2 Tetrahedral stereochemistry of atoms and allenes

  • Prefix
    • t
    • m

4.4 Isotopic layer

  • Prefix
    • i
    • h
    • b
    • t
    • m
    • s

item.title: Docs: Molecular Similarity
item.description:
Fragment Position - Based

Tanimoto Similarity

  • The Tanimoto coefficient is the ratio of the number of fragment positions shared by the molecules divided by the fragment positions set by either molecule.
  • The Tanimoto score ranges from 0 (no similarity) to 1 (identical molecules)

  • Calculation Example

Shape-Based

Distance Geometry

Conformer Generation

  • Balloon
  • Confab
  • Frog2
  • RDKit
  • MOE

Matched Molecular Pairs (MMP) Analysis

  • The mining and statistical analysis of transformations and their impact on properties of interest (e.g. solubility or activity)。
